title: IN-2: Force Layout Integration
description:
Integrate the live force-layout engine with real artifact creation, movement, relationship changes, and background-result flows.

SCOPE:
- Add newly created/successfully persisted artifacts to the simulation once they are eligible to participate.
- Reheat the layout when cards, edges, or research-result artifacts change the semantic structure materially.
- Publish the settled-layout signal/signature that topology, synthesis, snapshot, replay, and recovery beads consume.
- Preserve stable artifact identity while the layout layer manipulates DOM transforms and later syncs settled positions back to app state.
- Emit structured logs for reheats, settled signatures, and the event that caused each layout run.

USER VALUE:
- The live workspace keeps organizing itself coherently as new information arrives instead of only working in a mock-data sandbox.
design:
Live-layout integration contract:
- The simulation is not mock-only after this bead; live artifact creation and relationship changes must drive it.
- Result cards from research should join the same layout ecology rather than appearing as a detached list.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Live card creation feeds real artifacts into the simulation after ghost solidification.
2. Relevant structural changes (new cards, new edges, research-result cascades) reheat the simulation and yield a new settled signature when appropriate.
3. Settled-layout signals are available to topology, synthesis, snapshot, replay, and recovery flows.
4. Stable artifact identity is preserved through live simulation and settle cycles.
5. Structured logs capture reheat causes, node/edge counts, and settled signatures.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Emit explicit layout-settled signals/signatures that downstream beads can consume for topology analysis, synthesis triggering, snapshot recording, and replay fidelity.
- Preserve stable artifact identity while the DOM is manipulated directly during ticks so drag/replay/test selectors do not churn.
- Reheat and settle behavior should be deterministic enough that users can trust the semantic clustering rather than reading it as jitter.

title: IN-6: Replay Integration
description:
Wire live state snapshots to the replay UI.
- Every tool call, drag, and layout settle creates a snapshot
- Replay scrubber shows actual session history
- Playing back compresses session into time-lapse

Acceptance: After a full interaction (5+ cards, research, calendar), hit replay. 10-second time-lapse shows entire session evolution.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A full golden-path session records and replays snapshots for tool calls, drag-driven edges, layout settles, synthesis cards, research jobs/results, and calendar confirmation artifacts when those events occur.
2. Replay preserves stable artifact identity and interpolated movement instead of remounting everything as anonymous cards.
3. A 10-second playback of the golden path remains smooth, ordered, and visually faithful both immediately after the session and after restore/reload.
4. Replay can surface heterogeneous artifact types and edges clearly enough that a user can trust it as a history of the session, not just an animation gimmick.
5. Logs record snapshot counts, time span, artifact/edge counts, and replay start/end timing so regressions are diagnosable.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- The replay target is the full golden-path session, not just card creation. Final acceptance should include synthesis cards, job nodes, research-result cards, calendar confirmation cards, and any created edges when those events exist in the source session.

title: BE-4: move_artifact Handler
description:
Implement the `move_artifact` tool handler that lets the model reposition existing artifacts using semantic placement instructions instead of brittle raw coordinates.

SCOPE:
- Validate `artifact_id`, placement mode, and any placement-specific parameters (`near_artifact_id`, `x`, `y`).
- Support the full baseline placement enum set: `near_artifact`, `viewport_center`, `viewport_left`, `viewport_right`, `viewport_top`, `viewport_bottom`, and `coordinates`.
- Resolve the new position deterministically and clamp/validate obviously broken coordinate input.
- Persist the new position, notify the frontend, and return `{ artifact_id, status: "moved", position }` as a plain object.
- Emit structured logs for placement mode, reference artifacts, coordinate validation/clamping, and final resolved position.

USER VALUE:
- Model-driven rearrangement feels intentional and legible rather than random or error-prone.
design:
Movement contract:
- Use semantic placement first; raw coordinates are the fallback, not the default model interface.
- Viewport-relative placements should map cleanly onto the shared `(0,0)`-centered canvas model.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Valid `move_artifact` calls update the artifact position and notify the frontend.
2. All documented baseline placement modes are supported.
3. Invalid references or malformed coordinates fail clearly without corrupting artifact state.
4. The handler returns a plain-object response with the new position.
5. Structured logs capture placement mode, validation decisions, and resolved positions.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Cover the full spec placement enum set: near_artifact, viewport_center, viewport_left, viewport_right, viewport_top, viewport_bottom, and coordinates.
- Log placement-resolution decisions and clamp/validate coordinate inputs so invalid tool arguments fail clearly.

title: BE-8: Topology Analysis
description:
Compute and inject topology analysis after the force layout settles so the agent can read the spatial structure it created.

SCOPE:
- Analyze settled artifact positions for tight clusters, loose clusters, and isolates using the distance thresholds from the plan.
- Require a settled-layout signal/signature from the force-layout layer rather than running blindly on intermediate tick positions.
- Generate a stable topology signature/hash so identical structure is not re-injected repeatedly.
- Format the injected analysis in the documented `[TOPOLOGY_ANALYSIS]` style.
- Emit structured logs for cluster detection, isolate detection, signature changes, injection decisions, and skipped/no-op cases.

USER VALUE:
- The agent can derive emergent spatial insight from the canvas instead of only restating text that already exists.
design:
Reference injection shape from the plan:
`[TOPOLOGY_ANALYSIS] Layout settled. 12 artifacts on canvas.`
Then enumerate tight clusters, loose clusters, isolates, and a possible insight hint about shared root causes vs independent workstreams.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Settled layouts produce tight-cluster, loose-cluster, and isolate analysis in the documented coordinate model.
2. Topology analysis waits for a real settle/freeze signal instead of firing on transient tick positions.
3. Meaningfully unchanged topology is deduplicated via a signature/hash.
4. Injected topology context is structured enough that later speech/synthesis can reference cluster and isolate behavior.
5. Structured logs capture cluster/isolate counts, signature decisions, and injection outcomes.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Include loose-cluster detection from the spec, not just tight clusters and isolates.
- Compute and compare a topology signature/hash so injections are based on meaningful structural change, not merely rerunning the same analysis.
- Run topology analysis only after the live layout has actually settled or the freeze timeout has elapsed.

title: DY-2: Deploy Script
description:
Create scripts/deploy.sh for bonus points (+0.2).
- Automated deployment script per spec §2.4
- Sets env vars, runs gcloud deploy command
- Include in repo root

Acceptance: ./scripts/deploy.sh deploys the app to Cloud Run successfully.
design:
The script should be the executable form of DY-1, not a toy example. It should:
- check env vars first,
- run the canonical `gcloud run deploy` command,
- surface the deployed URL clearly,
- and remain aligned with the smoke-test/demo/submission workflow.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `scripts/deploy.sh` validates required environment variables before attempting deployment and fails loudly when any required value is missing.
2. The script codifies the same Cloud Run flags and environment contract as DY-1, including `--timeout=3600` and `--min-instances=1`.
3. Running the script prints the resulting service URL/revision information and exits non-zero on deployment failure.
4. The script is safe to rerun for the same service and does not silently drift from the documented deploy path.
5. The script is good enough to serve both as a bonus deliverable and as the actual operator path for deployment.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- The deploy script should codify the exact Cloud Run flags/env wiring from the final deployment bead and be safe to rerun.
- It should surface missing required environment variables loudly instead of silently deploying a broken service.

title: DM-3: Submit to Devpost
description:
Complete and submit the Devpost entry with every required asset, deadline guardrail, and testing instruction already resolved.

SCOPE:
- Assemble the final category choice, description, public GitHub URL, public demo video URL, cloud-proof asset, architecture diagram, testing instructions, and any bonus links that are ready.
- Verify all public links/assets in a fresh/incognito context before submitting.
- Keep the 4:00 PM PDT buffer as the real deadline guardrail rather than flirting with the hard cutoff.
- Ensure testing instructions explain mic permissions, desktop/browser expectations, known limitations, and the Live-API-unavailable fallback note.

USER VALUE:
- The product is not only built; it is actually submitted correctly and credibly.
design:
Pre-submit checklist to preserve from the plan:
- Devpost category = Live Agents.
- Public GitHub repo URL works.
- Public YouTube demo URL works in incognito.
- Cloud proof asset is attached separately.
- Architecture diagram is uploaded.
- Testing Instructions explain mic permissions, desktop-browser expectations, known limitations, and the Live-API-unavailable fallback note.
- Deployed URL loads without errors.
- Bonus links (blog, GDG, deploy script mention) are included if ready.
- Submit by 4:00 PM PDT buffer, not the hard 5:00 PM deadline.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The Devpost entry contains the correct category, description, public GitHub URL, public YouTube demo URL, cloud-proof asset, architecture diagram, and testing instructions.
2. Public URLs/assets are verified in an incognito/fresh-browser context before submission.
3. Testing instructions include mic permissions, desktop/browser expectations, known limitations, and the Live-API fallback note.
4. Bonus links/assets are included only if ready and do not endanger the deadline.
5. Submission is completed before the 4:00 PM PDT buffer deadline.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- The final submission bead should carry the pre-submit checklist from spec §14: public YouTube, public GitHub, deployed URL loads, all Devpost fields filled, testing instructions present, and submission complete before the 4:00 PM PDT buffer.
- Bonus items are still worth including, but they must never cause the team to miss the submission deadline.

title: IN-1: End-to-End Audio + Tool Wiring
description:
Wire everything together for the core loop: speak → Gemini → tool calls → cards on canvas.
- Browser audio capture → WebSocket → backend → Gemini Live API
- Gemini tool calls → tool executor → Firestore → frontend notification
- Frontend receives notification → renders ghost card → solidifies
- Gemini audio response → backend → browser → playback
- System prompt loaded, agent responds as Eureka persona
- Verify: speaking about a project creates cards SILENTLY (no agent narration during creation)

Acceptance: Speak naturally about 3 project concerns. 3+ ghost cards appear silently during speech. Cards solidify. After pause, agent speaks one brief insight. No card creation narration.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A user can connect, arm the mic or use typed fallback, and visibly understand system state through controls, transcript fallback, and connection indicator while the live loop runs.
2. Speaking a natural planning burst produces 3-5 card-creation tool calls, frontend ghost-card renders, and persisted artifacts without narration during the creation window.
3. Cards solidify after 300ms and the agent speaks only after the pause/turn boundary, delivering a brief insight instead of narrating tool activity.
4. Structured logs correlate user input timing, tool-call timing, frontend notification timing, and any emitted output audio so silence during creation can be verified directly.
5. If the live loop fails, the UI keeps enough visible state and diagnostics that the user is not left with a silent, empty canvas.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Treat the core loop as a trust-building experience, not just plumbing: the connection indicator, transcript fallback, and controls must all be present so a user can understand what the system is doing while testing the live path.
- Instrument the silence requirement directly by logging whether any output audio was emitted during card-creation windows.

title: CU-14: Research Job Streaming Progress
description:
Track the spec’s P2 streaming-progress enhancement separately from the baseline job-node work.

SCOPE:
- Extend the research job node so it can show staged progress updates while the research runner is active.
- Progress should communicate phases such as queued, searching, synthesizing, and writing findings.
- The UI must remain compatible with the simpler running/complete/failed job-node states from the baseline build.
- Progress updates should be optional and degrade cleanly to the simpler pulsing-running state.

ACCEPTANCE:
1. A running research job can display stage-based progress updates without replacing the baseline job-node UI.
2. Progress updates stream in order and stop once the job completes or fails.
3. Completion/failure states override the progress indicator cleanly.
4. Replay can represent the progress transitions if snapshot granularity allows.
5. The feature degrades to the simpler running pulse when streaming progress is unavailable.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A running research job can display staged progress updates without replacing the baseline job-node UI.
2. Progress updates stream in order and stop once the job completes or fails.
3. Completion and failure states override the progress indicator cleanly.
4. The feature degrades back to the simpler running pulse when granular progress is unavailable.
5. Any implementation extends UI/e2e coverage with structured logs for progress-stage ordering, completion override, and replay compatibility.

title: IN-7: Workspace Restore + Reload Recovery
description:
Implement the save/restore experience required by spec §§4.3, 9, and 11.3 so a refresh or revisit does not destroy the user’s thinking space.

SCOPE:
- Establish a durable workspace-identity flow for local/demo use (query param, stored ID, or equivalent deterministic bootstrap).
- On initial load, fetch the workspace plus persisted artifacts, edges, jobs, and snapshots from Firestore.
- Hydrate the live canvas with heterogeneous artifact types, not just plain cards: synthesis cards, research job nodes, research-result cards, calendar confirmation cards, and edges.
- Rehydrate replay history from persisted snapshots when available so replay survives refreshes.
- Reconcile hydrated state with the live WebSocket/session startup sequence so a reconnect does not duplicate artifacts or briefly flash an empty canvas.
- Handle empty workspaces, partially written records, and stale/malformed documents gracefully with structured diagnostics.
- Emit structured logs for hydration timings, document counts, dropped records, and restore success/failure.

USER VALUE:
- Users can reload or reopen the workspace without losing the visual structure the agent created with them.
- Replay remains a trustworthy record of the session rather than an in-memory-only effect.
acceptance_criteria:
1. After a golden-path session, reloading the app restores the current artifacts, edges, and heterogeneous artifact visuals from Firestore.
2. Persisted replay snapshots rehydrate into the replay UI when snapshot data exists.
3. A brand-new or empty workspace loads a clean canvas without errors.
4. Partial or malformed persisted data is skipped or surfaced with actionable logs instead of crashing the app.
5. The restored workspace is immediately usable for continued live interaction, not read-only.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Treat persistence as a full user experience, not just CRUD plumbing: the first frame after refresh should already reflect the user’s prior thinking state.
- Preserve stable artifact IDs and edge IDs through hydration so replay interpolation, DOM hooks, and future tool calls remain coherent.
- If replay fidelity requires optional viewport/edge metadata beyond the base spec fields, store it alongside snapshots without weakening the required schema.

title: BE-14: delete_artifact Tool + Single-Level Undo
description:
Capture the spec’s deferred deletion/undo capability without making it part of the demo-critical path.

SCOPE:
- Implement a delete_artifact tool that removes an artifact and any invalidated relationships safely.
- Add a single-level undo path so the most recent delete can be reversed.
- Decide explicitly whether deletion is soft-delete/tombstone or hard-delete, but preserve replay/history integrity either way.
- Ensure snapshots and canvas replay can represent the delete and undo transitions.
- Prevent accidental cascading corruption when deleting grouped, linked, or replayed artifacts.

ACCEPTANCE:
1. delete_artifact removes the targeted artifact and updates dependent edges/state consistently.
2. The most recent delete can be undone once.
3. Replay/history remain coherent after delete and undo operations.
4. Duplicate undo or invalid delete requests fail safely with clear errors/logs.
5. The baseline build remains unaffected when this upgrade is not implemented.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `delete_artifact` removes the targeted artifact and reconciles affected edges/groups/state consistently.
2. The most recent delete can be undone exactly once without corrupting the rest of the workspace.
3. Replay/history remain coherent across delete and undo transitions.
4. Invalid delete/undo requests fail safely with clear errors and logs.
5. Any implementation extends backend/e2e coverage with structured logs for delete side effects, undo state, and replay persistence.

title: TS-1: Audio Pipeline Unit Tests
description:
Comprehensive unit tests for the entire audio pipeline with detailed logging.
Test framework: Vitest. File: __tests__/audio-pipeline.test.ts

=== TEST SUITE 1: AudioWorklet Processor (AU-1) ===

Test 1.1: Downsample 48kHz → 16kHz
- Input: 480 Float32 samples at 48kHz (10ms of audio)
- Expected: 160 Int16 samples at 16kHz
- Verify: output length = input length * (16000/48000)
- Verify: values are correctly averaged across 3-sample windows (48k/16k = 3)
- Log: input sample count, output sample count, ratio, first 10 output values

Test 1.2: Downsample 44100Hz → 16kHz
- Input: 441 Float32 samples at 44.1kHz (10ms of audio)
- Expected: 160 Int16 samples at 16kHz (with interpolation)
- Verify: output length is correct
- Verify: no aliasing artifacts (output values within expected range)
- Log: interpolation points used, output sample count

Test 1.3: Float32 → Int16 conversion
- Input: [0.0, 1.0, -1.0, 0.5, -0.5, 0.999969] as Float32
- Expected: [0, 32767, -32768, 16383, -16384, 32767] as Int16
- Verify: clamping at boundaries (values > 1.0 clamp to 32767, < -1.0 clamp to -32768)
- Log: each input → output pair

Test 1.4: Empty input handling
- Input: empty Float32Array
- Expected: empty Int16Array, no errors
- Log: confirmed graceful empty handling

Test 1.5: Chunk size consistency
- Process 100 consecutive 10ms chunks
- Verify: total output samples = expected for 1 second at 16kHz (16000)
- Log: per-chunk sizes, cumulative total

=== TEST SUITE 2: Audio Playback Resampling (AU-4) ===

Test 2.1: Resample 24kHz → 48kHz
- Input: 240 Int16 samples at 24kHz (10ms)
- Expected: 480 Float32 samples at 48kHz (2x upsample)
- Verify: linear interpolation between samples
- Log: input/output lengths, first 10 interpolated values

Test 2.2: Resample 24kHz → 44100Hz
- Input: 240 Int16 samples at 24kHz (10ms)
- Expected: 441 Float32 samples at 44100Hz (1.8375x upsample with interpolation)
- Verify: output length correct, values properly interpolated
- Log: ratio, interpolation details

Test 2.3: Int16 → Float32 conversion for playback
- Input: [0, 32767, -32768, 16384] as Int16
- Expected: [0.0, ~1.0, ~-1.0, ~0.5] as Float32
- Verify: normalized to [-1.0, 1.0] range
- Log: each conversion

Test 2.4: Buffer queuing
- Queue 5 audio chunks rapidly
- Verify: chunks play sequentially without gaps or overlap
- Verify: queue length tracking is accurate
- Log: queue state after each enqueue, play timing

Test 2.5: Interruption handling
- Queue 3 chunks, then trigger interruption
- Verify: playback stops immediately
- Verify: queue is cleared
- Verify: pending chunks are discarded
- Log: queue state before/after interruption, timing

=== TEST SUITE 3: AudioCapture Wrapper ===

Test 3.1: Start/stop lifecycle
- Mock AudioContext and AudioWorklet
- Call start() → verify AudioContext created, mic requested, processor registered
- Call stop() → verify tracks stopped, context closed
- Log: lifecycle events with timestamps

Test 3.2: onChunk callback
- Mock processor posting messages
- Verify: onChunk fires with correct ArrayBuffer data
- Log: chunk sizes, callback count

=== LOGGING REQUIREMENTS ===
Every test must:
1. Log test name and description at start
2. Log input data summary (type, length, first few values)
3. Log expected vs actual output
4. Log PASS/FAIL with specific assertion details
5. On failure: log full input/output for debugging

Use console.log with structured format:
  [TS-1] [Suite.Test] Description | input: ... | expected: ... | actual: ... | PASS/FAIL
acceptance_criteria:
1. The Bun/Vitest audio suite covers capture DSP, playback resampling, queueing, interruption flush, and AudioCapture lifecycle behavior.
2. Tests validate both 48kHz and 44.1kHz capture inputs plus 24kHz playback resampling to common system rates.
3. Structured logs expose enough numeric detail to debug sample-count or interpolation regressions quickly.
4. Pure DSP helpers and the AudioWorklet-facing implementation stay in verified parity.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Add parity tests between the pure DSP helpers and the AudioWorklet-facing implementation so audio math cannot silently diverge between runtime and tests.
- Include queue-depth/interruption assertions for the playback path, not just sample conversion math.

title: DY-1: Cloud Run Deployment
description:
Deploy to Cloud Run per spec §5.6.
- Create Dockerfile (or use buildpacks)
- gcloud run deploy eureka-canvas --source . --region us-central1 --allow-unauthenticated --timeout=3600 --min-instances=1
- Set env vars for Gemini API key, model name, GCP project
- Verify WebSocket works on deployed instance

Acceptance: App is accessible at Cloud Run URL. Audio pipeline works on deployed version.
design:
Exact deploy contract to preserve from the plan:
`gcloud run deploy eureka-canvas --source . --region us-central1 --allow-unauthenticated --timeout=3600 --min-instances=1 --set-env-vars "GEMINI_API_KEY=$GEMINI_API_KEY,GEMINI_MODEL=$GEMINI_MODEL,GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT=$GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT,GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID=$GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID,GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=$GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET,GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URI=$GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URI,GOOGLE_REFRESH_TOKEN=$GOOGLE_REFRESH_TOKEN"`
Keep WebSocket viability, restore/reconnect verification, and deployed smoke testing tied to this command path.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Deployment uses Cloud Run with the exact demo-safe baseline from the plan: `--allow-unauthenticated`, `--timeout=3600`, `--min-instances=1`, correct region, and the required environment configuration for Gemini, Firestore/project, and OAuth-backed calendar behavior.
2. The deployed URL loads successfully, health checks pass, WebSocket handshake succeeds, and the live audio/tool loop is verified against the deployed build rather than only local dev.
3. Deployed behavior preserves the demo-critical trust surfaces: connection indicator, transcript fallback, restore/reload behavior, and reconnect/session-resumption behavior.
4. The final deployed URL, service name, region, and environment checklist are recorded so the smoke-test, cloud-proof, demo, and submission beads can reuse them without rediscovery.
5. Deployment happens only after the local verification gate is green (`check` + unit suites + browser e2e), not as an exploratory spike.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Treat this as the final demo-ready deployment bead, not an early spike deploy.
- Deployment should happen only after the full effective-P0 integration set and local e2e verification are green.
- Verify the deployed service keeps WebSocket sessions alive with --timeout=3600 and avoids cold-start surprises with --min-instances=1.
- Capture the exact deployed URL and environment shape needed for the smoke-test and demo-recording beads.

title: DL-1: Architecture Diagram
description:
Create the polished architecture diagram that judges can parse in seconds while still reflecting the real shipped baseline.

SCOPE:
- Produce a clean SVG/PNG architecture asset for the repo/demo.
- Show the actual server-proxy architecture: Browser ↔ Cloud Run ↔ Gemini Live API, plus Firestore persistence, Google Calendar, and the separate grounded research call.
- Label the SDK/platform features that matter to judges: native audio, Proactive Audio, thinking budget, session resumption/sliding-window compression, and the blocking-tool baseline with future FunctionResponseScheduling upgrade path called out honestly.
- Save the asset in the repo where demo/blog/submission work can reuse it.

USER VALUE:
- Judges and users can understand the product architecture quickly without guessing what is real versus aspirational.
design:
Diagram elements to preserve from the plan:
- Browser: canvas UI, audio capture/playback, transcript, replay, drag interactions.
- Cloud Run: WebSocket proxy, tool executor, state injection, replay/snapshot persistence, recovery/session resumption.
- Gemini Live API: native audio, proactive audio, thinking budget, blocking tool path.
- Separate grounded research call.
- Firestore + Google Calendar.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A polished architecture asset exists in the repo and is usable in the demo/blog/submission flow.
2. The diagram reflects the real shipped baseline architecture and does not overclaim backlog-only behavior.
3. The separate grounded research call, Firestore persistence, Calendar API, and session/recovery features are all visible.
4. SDK/platform features judges care about are labeled clearly.
5. The diagram is readable quickly enough to work in a short demo segment.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- The diagram should reflect the final architecture decisions: server proxy, blocking tool execution in the baseline build, separate generateContent research call with Google Search grounding, Firestore persistence, Calendar API, session resumption/sliding-window compression, and on-demand snapshot grounding as an upgrade path.
- Label the exact SDK concepts judges care about without implying features that are still backlog-only.

title: BE-15: create_dag Tool + Task DAG Rendering
description:
Preserve the spec’s P2 task-DAG capability as an explicitly tracked upgrade instead of letting it disappear behind generic edge rendering.

SCOPE:
- Implement the documented create_dag tool contract from spec §7.2.
- Create dag_node artifacts plus the corresponding directed edges.
- Render the resulting graph in a readable task-DAG layout that is visually distinct from loose semantic card clustering.
- Ensure DAG nodes/edges persist, replay, and coexist with the rest of the canvas model.
- Validate edge references and prevent invalid cycles introduced by malformed tool input.

ACCEPTANCE:
1. create_dag can generate multiple nodes and directed edges from a single tool call.
2. DAG nodes render with readable structure instead of collapsing into generic overlapping cards.
3. Directed relationships remain stable through reload and replay.
4. Invalid node indexes or malformed edge definitions fail with clear errors/logs.
5. The baseline demo path remains unaffected when this upgrade is not yet implemented.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `create_dag` can generate multiple DAG nodes and directed edges from a single tool call.
2. DAG nodes render with a readable DAG-specific structure instead of collapsing into generic overlapping cards.
3. Directed relationships remain stable through reload, replay, and mixed-canvas coexistence with normal artifacts.
4. Invalid node indexes, malformed edge definitions, or cycle-breaking validations fail safely with clear errors and logs.
5. Any implementation extends backend/UI/e2e coverage with structured logs for DAG generation, persistence, and restore/replay fidelity.

title: BE-1: Firestore CRUD
description:
Workspace and artifact persistence per spec §9.
- lib/firestore/ module
- firebase-admin SDK initialization
- Functions: createWorkspace(), getWorkspace(), createArtifact(), updateArtifact(), getArtifacts(), createEdge(), getEdges()
- Data models match spec §9 exactly
- Timestamps as Firestore server timestamps
- Convert Firestore Timestamps to ISO strings before returning to tool responses (spec §7.1 note about non-serializable values)

Acceptance: Can create a workspace, add artifacts, retrieve them. Firestore console shows correct data structure.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Persistence helpers exist for every spec-backed model needed by the shipped plan: Workspace, Artifact, Edge, Job, and StateSnapshot.
2. Stored field names, status enums, artifact types, and timestamp handling match the spec closely enough that later tool/replay/recovery beads do not need schema translation.
3. Firestore-specific values are converted into plain serializable response objects before anything reaches the Live-session tool-response path.
4. CRUD and query helpers support cross-workspace isolation, heterogeneous artifact restore, job lifecycle persistence, and replay snapshot retrieval.
5. Structured logs capture collection name, document IDs, counts, latencies, and skipped/malformed-record handling for restore/debug workflows.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Expand the persistence layer to cover the full spec data model: Workspace, Artifact, Edge, Job, and StateSnapshot CRUD/helpers.
- State snapshots may store optional replay-fidelity metadata such as edges and viewport state in addition to the required artifact array, as long as the spec-required fields remain present.
- Provide serialization helpers that strip/convert Firestore-specific types before anything reaches the live-session tool-response path.

title: BE-6: Canvas State Injection
description:
Inject the current canvas state into the live session whenever the model needs fresh spatial context, without wasting context budget on redundant snapshots.

SCOPE:
- Format `[CANVAS_STATE]` messages using the shared viewport metadata (`center`, `zoom`, `size`) and current artifact state in the spec’s coordinate model.
- Trigger injection adaptively: after tool-call responses, before spatial-command handling, after user drag, after layout settles, and during idle intervals only when the state has changed.
- Track a dedupe/signature hash so unchanged state is not re-injected unnecessarily.
- Include enough artifact detail that spatial references remain grounded without requiring continuous screenshots.
- Emit structured logs for injection triggers, signature comparisons, payload size, and delivery outcomes.

USER VALUE:
- The agent stays grounded in the live canvas instead of operating on stale spatial assumptions.
design:
Reference payload shape from the plan:
`[CANVAS_STATE] Viewport: center(0,0), zoom:1.0, size:1200x800. Artifacts: [...]`
Preserve the same semantic structure even if exact serialization details vary slightly in code.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `[CANVAS_STATE]` payloads include the current viewport center, zoom, visible size, and artifact state in the shared coordinate model.
2. State injection runs on the adaptive schedule from the plan, including the pre-spatial-command path.
3. Unchanged state is deduplicated instead of wasting context window budget.
4. Delivery failures or skipped injections are logged clearly.
5. Structured logs capture trigger source, signature/hash decisions, and payload-size information.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Include the adaptive trigger that happens immediately before spatial-command handling, not just after writes/settles/idle intervals.
- Encode viewport center, zoom, and visible-area metadata exactly as the spec requires so spatial references stay grounded in the shared coordinate system.
- Keep the schedule adaptive rather than reverting to a wasteful fixed interval; unchanged state should not consume context budget.

title: CU-7: Canvas Replay UI
description:
Build the replay surface that lets users scrub through the evolution of their thinking instead of treating the session as disposable.

FILES:
- components/CanvasReplay.tsx
- any supporting replay-mode state/control wiring in the canvas layer

SCOPE:
- Render a bottom-of-canvas replay scrubber with play/pause and speed controls.
- Accept real snapshot history and interpolate artifact movement between snapshots instead of hard-cutting everything.
- Handle heterogeneous artifact types and edges during playback: normal cards, synthesis cards, job nodes, research results, calendar confirmation cards, and relationship overlays when snapshot data contains them.
- Preserve stable artifact identity during scrub/play so replay does not remount everything as anonymous nodes.
- Support the 10-second “session collapse” playback used by the demo closer while still allowing manual scrubbing.
- Expose stable selectors/hooks for replay mode, current timestamp, scrub position, and playback state.

USER VALUE:
- Replay turns the session into a visible history of thought, which is one of the most compelling user-facing moments in the whole system.
design:
Replay contract from the plan:
- Manual scrub + autoplay.
- Time-compressed “5 minutes of thinking in 10 seconds” closer for the demo.
- Replay should read as a truthful session history, not just an animation gimmick.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Given real snapshot data, the scrubber can enter replay mode, scrub manually, and autoplay the session history.
2. Artifact positions interpolate smoothly between snapshots and artifact appearance/disappearance is handled intentionally rather than abruptly.
3. Heterogeneous artifact types and edges remain readable during playback when the source history contains them.
4. A 10-second playback of the golden path is smooth and trustworthy enough for demo use.
5. Stable selectors/hooks exist for replay mode, current timestamp, playback state, and scrub position.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Replay UI should be ready for heterogeneous artifact types, not only plain cards: synthesis cards, job nodes, research-result cards, calendar confirmation cards, and edges when replay data contains them.
- Preserve artifact identity across scrubber movement so DOM/test hooks and interpolated motion remain stable.

title: BN-1: GDG Membership Signup
description:
HUMAN-ONLY TASK: Sign up for Google Developer Groups (GDG) for +0.2 bonus points.

STEPS:
1. Go to https://developers.google.com/community/gdg
2. Create/update GDG profile
3. Copy profile URL
4. Add profile URL to Devpost submission (DM-3)

Per spec §2.4: +0.2 bonus points for active GDG profile linked in submission.
On a 5-point scale, total bonus (blog + deploy script + GDG) = 1.0 = 20% boost.
acceptance_criteria:
1. An active GDG profile exists and its public URL has been captured.
2. The URL is stored where DM-3 can include it without re-discovery.
3. The signup/profile task is complete early enough that it does not jeopardize submission timing.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Treat this as a submission-readiness bead, not an optional afterthought; the URL needs to be ready before the final Devpost pass.
- Capture the exact public profile URL in the issue/thread context so the human submitter does not have to search for it under deadline pressure.

title: CU-5: SVG Edge Rendering
description:
Render the structural SVG edge layer that makes relationships visible immediately when the agent or the user creates them.

FILES:
- components/DagEdge.tsx
- the SVG overlay/layering logic in the canvas surface

SCOPE:
- Render dependency edges as arrows between source/target artifacts using an SVG layer that does not block card interaction.
- Keep the implementation ready for additional edge types such as membership without hard-coding only one visual path forever.
- Recompute anchors when cards move because of drag, force-layout ticks/settles, replay interpolation, restore hydration, or viewport changes.
- Expose stable edge identifiers/hooks so replay, tests, and future multi-view/DAG work can reuse them.
- Ensure edge rendering remains visually subtle but legible at demo scale.

USER VALUE:
- Relationship creation feels immediate and concrete instead of hidden in backend state.
design:
Edge-layer contract:
- Dependency arrows are the immediate requirement for drag-as-communication.
- The overlay should sit visually beneath cards but update in lockstep with their movement.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Given two artifacts and a dependency edge, a visible arrow renders between the correct source and target anchors.
2. The edge updates when either artifact moves because of drag, force-layout, replay, or restore.
3. The SVG overlay does not interfere with card dragging or control interaction.
4. Stable IDs/test hooks exist for edge rendering assertions and replay/history reuse.
5. The implementation can support additional edge types later without rewriting the entire overlay primitive.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Baseline demo value comes from dependency edges, but the edge layer should still be designed as a reusable relationship overlay because later roadmap beads need membership/DAG relationships too.

title: TS-4: E2E Integration Test Script
acceptance_criteria:
1. `scripts/e2e-test.sh` runs via Bun-only commands, executes unit suites first, then launches browser-driven end-to-end coverage.
2. The harness supports deterministic mock mode and live mode.
3. Scenarios cover the golden path plus reload/restore recovery and connection-loss/session-resumption behavior.
4. Every run emits structured per-step logs and retains timestamped artifacts such as traces, screenshots, or videos for failures.
5. The final report is concise enough to use as a pre-demo confidence gate.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Use a real browser-automation harness (Playwright or equivalent) for DOM/CSS/drag/replay verification rather than relying only on headless Vitest integration.
- Support at least two execution modes: deterministic mock mode for CI/local confidence and live mode for final human-verified smoke runs.
- The shell script should run unit suites first, then browser e2e, and persist logs/traces/screenshots/videos for failures.
- Add a reconnect/connection-loss scenario so the connection-status flow is exercised before demo recording.
- Add a refresh/reload restore scenario that verifies persisted workspace and replay state survive a page restart.
- Retain a terse scenario summary plus pointers to captured artifacts so failures are diagnosable under time pressure.

title: TS-5: Deployed Smoke Test Script
description:
Add a post-deployment smoke-test script so the team can validate the Cloud Run build before recording the demo or submitting.

FILES:
- scripts/smoke-deploy.sh
- any supporting test harness/config needed for scripted checks

SCOPE:
- Accept the deployed base URL as input and log every step with timestamps.
- Verify health endpoint reachability, app HTML load, and WebSocket handshake success.
- Check visible readiness signals that matter for judges: connection indicator state, transcript fallback presence, and replay entry point if enabled.
- Support a lightweight live/manual checkpoint section for audio/calendar/research features that cannot be fully automated safely against production.
- Write logs to a timestamped artifact so failures are diagnosable after the fact.

ACCEPTANCE:
1. Running the script against a deployed URL produces a timestamped smoke-test log.
2. The script fails fast when the health endpoint, app shell, or WebSocket handshake is broken.
3. The script records enough detail to distinguish app failure from environment/config failure.
4. The output is concise enough to use before demo recording and Devpost submission.
5. The script becomes part of the final pre-submit verification path.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Running the script against a deployed URL produces a timestamped smoke-test log and retained diagnostic artifacts.
2. The script fails fast when the health endpoint, app shell, or WebSocket handshake is broken.
3. The script verifies visible readiness signals that matter for judges, including connection indicator state and transcript fallback presence.
4. The deployed check includes a lightweight refresh/restore probe and records whether reconnect behavior remains healthy when exercised.
5. The output is concise enough to use before demo recording and Devpost submission.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Include a quick refresh/restore check after the initial page load so deployed persistence is exercised, not just the first HTML render.
- If the deployed environment supports it safely, exercise a lightweight reconnect path and log whether the indicator returns to Connected cleanly.
- Persist timestamped logs plus at least one screenshot/HTML artifact so remote failures can be diagnosed after the run.

title: BE-10: State Snapshot Recording
description:
Record replay snapshots whenever the workspace meaningfully changes so replay, restore, and debugging can treat session history as first-class data.

SCOPE:
- Record snapshots on tool completion, user drag, and layout settle, using explicit trigger labels.
- Persist the required artifact history plus the optional replay-fidelity metadata that improves trust: edges and viewport state when available.
- Deep-clone snapshot data so later mutation does not corrupt history.
- Persist snapshots to Firestore and expose retrieval helpers for replay/restore flows.
- Emit structured logs for trigger source, snapshot IDs, counts, payload size, and persistence latency.

USER VALUE:
- Replay and restore become trustworthy records of a session, not fragile in-memory side effects.
design:
Snapshot contract:
- Required fields: timestamp, trigger, artifact history.
- Recommended extra fidelity: edges and viewport metadata when available.
- Replay/restore should consume the same persisted history rather than inventing a separate representation.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Snapshots are recorded on the documented trigger points with ordered timestamps and explicit trigger labels.
2. Snapshot payloads preserve artifact history and include edges/viewport metadata when available for higher replay fidelity.
3. Deep cloning prevents later mutation from changing already-recorded history.
4. Persisted snapshots can be retrieved for replay and restore flows.
5. Structured logs capture trigger source, snapshot IDs, counts, and write latency.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Persist enough replay fidelity for restore/replay trust: artifacts are mandatory, and edges/viewport metadata should be stored when available so the timeline reflects structural changes accurately.
- Snapshot recording must remain cheap and deterministic because it runs on tool completions, drag events, and layout settles.
- Emit structured logs for trigger source, artifact/edge counts, write latency, and snapshot IDs.

title: AU-1: AudioWorklet Capture Processor
acceptance_criteria:
1. AudioWorklet processor file exists at public/audio-processor.js (vanilla JS)
2. Calling start() requests mic permission, creates AudioContext, registers processor
3. onChunk fires with ArrayBuffer containing Int16 PCM at 16kHz
4. Chunk sizes consistent (~1600 samples per 100ms chunk)
5. Downsampling handles both 48kHz and 44100Hz input rates
6. Float32 → Int16 conversion clamps correctly at boundaries
7. stop() releases mic, closes context
8. Tab visibility handler resumes AudioContext on re-focus
9. Console log confirms: "AudioCapture started, native rate: {rate}Hz, target: 16000Hz"
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Avoid Array.push(...input) on long-lived audio streams; use a typed-array accumulator or ring-buffer approach to reduce GC pressure and audio dropouts.
- Mirror the DSP logic in testable pure utilities so AU-1/TS-1 can validate the exact math outside the AudioWorklet runtime.
- Normalize to mono explicitly if the browser provides multiple channels.
- Log native sample rate, chunk duration, output sample count, and dropped/late chunk counters for debugging real hardware issues.

title: AU-3: Gemini Live API Session
description:
Connect the backend to Gemini Live API with the exact baseline session contract the project will actually ship.

SCOPE:
- Use `@google/genai` with `apiVersion: "v1alpha"`.
- Verify the configured native-audio model resolves before downstream live beads proceed; log the exact resolved model name.
- Build the baseline session config: audio output modality, Eureka system prompt, BLOCKING tool declarations for `create_card`, `move_artifact`, `update_artifact`, `create_calendar_event`, and `start_research_job`, proactive-audio config, thinking budget 512, input/output transcription, session resumption, sliding-window compression, voice config, and `silenceDurationMs: 700`.
- Implement the event-handling surface the rest of the app depends on: tool calls, output-audio chunks, interruptions, turn completion, input/output transcription, session IDs/resume tokens, and reconnect metadata.
- Expose runtime/feature flags for proactive-audio enablement, demo silence tuning (700 default / 500 demo), and future NON_BLOCKING experiments without rewriting the session layer.
- Preserve the shipped fallback posture: BLOCKING tools are the baseline; proactive audio stays enabled only if it does not suppress tool calls; any FunctionResponseScheduling experiment is optional upgrade work.

USER VALUE:
- The live agent behaves consistently and silently during canvas updates instead of sounding like a generic narrator.
- Fallback switches exist before demo pressure hits, so the team can keep a working baseline if the API behaves badly.
design:
System-prompt directives that must be preserved in code/config:
- Identity: Eureka is sharp, concise, and spatially minded; it thinks in space rather than chatting abstractly.
- Never use assistant filler such as “Sure,” “I’d be happy to help,” or “as an AI.”
- NEVER narrate tool calls. The canvas update is the communication.
- Auto-crystallize aggressively: when the user expresses multiple concepts, create 3-5 cards per thought burst without waiting for permission.
- Synthesis: after 5+ artifacts and a pause, create at most one genuine `status:"synthesis"` insight card only when a real cross-card insight exists.
- Topology: when `[TOPOLOGY_ANALYSIS]` arrives, derive insight from cluster density/isolation instead of merely restating card text.
- Speaking policy: create silently during active speech; speak only after a pause or direct question, and speak insight rather than narration.
- Spatial awareness: honor `[CANVAS_STATE]` and `[CANVAS_UPDATE]` context, confirm spatial references briefly, and act.
- Interruptions: stop immediately, pivot cleanly, and only offer to resume if prior work was substantial.
- Research: launch `start_research_job`, then announce results when the injected system message delivers them.
- Calendar: call `create_calendar_event` and confirm briefly.
Fallback matrix to preserve from the plan:
- Default shipped path: blocking canvas/action tools + system-prompt silence rules.
- If proactive audio suppresses tool calls during natural thinking-aloud input, disable proactive audio and keep the rest of the session alive.
- Any NON_BLOCKING + SILENT experiment must log whether narration/duplicate audio regresses; if it does, keep the blocking baseline.
- Before final demo recording, audition Kore, Charon, Puck, Aoede, and Fenrir; keep the cleanest grounded voice, but the implementation default remains Kore until that choice is made.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The backend opens a Gemini Live session successfully using `@google/genai` with `apiVersion: "v1alpha"` and logs the exact resolved model name before downstream live beads proceed.
2. Session config includes the shipped baseline from the spec/AGENTS: Eureka persona/system prompt, audio modality, blocking tool declarations for `create_card`, `move_artifact`, `update_artifact`, `create_calendar_event`, and `start_research_job`, proactive-audio config, thinking budget 512, input/output transcription, session resumption, sliding-window compression, voice config, and `silenceDurationMs: 700`.
3. Runtime flags/config switches exist for proactive-audio enablement, silence-duration adjustment (700 default / 500 demo), and future NON_BLOCKING experiments without rewriting the session layer.
4. Event handling covers tool calls, audio output, interruptions, turn completion, input/output transcription, and resume/reconnect metadata with structured logs for each path.
5. The baseline behavior decision is documented in code/logs: blocking tools ship first, proactive audio stays enabled only if it does not suppress tool calls, and any future NON_BLOCKING experiment is treated as an upgrade path rather than a requirement.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- This bead must embed the full Eureka persona/system-prompt behavior from spec §§5.2 and 6, including: never narrate tool calls, auto-crystallize 3-5 cards per thought burst, topology-aware insight, synthesis behavior, spatial-awareness behavior, research and calendar behavior, and interrupt handling.
- Add feature flags/runtime switches for proactiveAudio, silenceDurationMs, and any future NON_BLOCKING experiment so the team can fall back quickly without rewriting the session layer.
- Verify the model name before other live-session beads proceed and log the exact model that resolved.
- Record the fallback decision matrix explicitly: blocking tools are the default build, and any future NON_BLOCKING + SILENT trial is an upgrade experiment with comparative logs.

title: AU-4: Audio Proxy Pipeline
description:
Implement the full browser↔backend↔Gemini audio transport loop across spec §§5.3, 10.2, and 12.

SCOPE:
- Stream 16kHz Int16 PCM chunks from the browser to the backend over WebSocket with enough metadata to trace ordering and latency.
- Forward those chunks to Gemini Live as `realtimeInput` audio blobs with the correct MIME type.
- Proxy 24kHz PCM output chunks from Gemini back to the browser and hand them to the playback queue.
- Resample 24kHz output to the browser’s AudioContext sample rate, queue sequential playback, and avoid gaps/overlap between chunks.
- On interruption or resume, stop active playback, clear the queue, discard stale turn audio, and prevent resumed sessions from replaying obsolete chunks.
- Maintain recent-input / last-speech timestamps that downstream silence-aware beads can consume for synthesis timing and silent-action verification.
- Emit structured logs for queue depth, chunk counts, round-trip timing, playback underruns, interruption flushes, dropped/late frames, and resample ratios.

USER VALUE:
- Voice interaction feels live and interruptible rather than laggy, overlapping, or haunted by stale audio.
- When audio does fail, the system leaves behind enough diagnostics and transcript context to understand what broke.
design:
Transport contract to preserve from the plan:
- Browser capture sends `audio/pcm;rate=16000` Int16 chunks to the backend.
- Gemini returns 24kHz PCM; browser playback resamples to the AudioContext rate rather than forcing a 24kHz AudioContext.
- Interruption handling is part of the audio path itself: stop playback, clear queue, discard stale chunks, and record the flush in logs.
- The backend and browser should both tag/log turn/session identifiers so audio issues can be correlated with tool-call and reconnect events.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Browser audio reaches Gemini and Gemini audio reaches the browser using the required raw-PCM formats and MIME types.
2. Playback runs through an AudioContext at the system sample rate with correct 24kHz→system-rate resampling and sequential queueing.
3. Speaking over the agent flushes active/pending playback immediately and prevents stale audio from replaying after interruption or reconnect.
4. The transport exposes recent-input timing and playback/queue telemetry that downstream silence-aware and recovery beads can consume.
5. Structured logs capture chunk counts, queue depth, latency, underruns, dropped frames, and interruption flush behavior clearly enough to debug real hardware/network failures.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Track queue depth, playback latency, interruption flush counts, and resample ratios in structured logs so audio regressions can be diagnosed quickly.
- Keep playback buffering deterministic enough that transcript fallback and connection-state UI can explain what happened when audio output fails.
- Preserve enough timing metadata that synthesis/silence logic can tell whether the user is actively speaking or has paused.

title: BE-7: Drag Detection → Context Injection + Edge Creation
description:
Turn user drag intent into deterministic structural updates plus model context, so drag-as-communication works even if the model never emits a follow-up tool call.

SCOPE:
- Receive drag-end data with artifact identity, origin/destination coordinates, and viewport context.
- Detect the nearest artifact and apply the documented proximity threshold (~80px) to decide whether the gesture expresses a relationship.
- When the gesture is relational, create or reuse the dependency edge immediately on the backend, persist the moved position, notify the frontend, and inject `[CANVAS_UPDATE]` context with `turnComplete: true` so the model can interpret the relationship verbally.
- When the gesture is not relational, persist the moved position and inject a simple position update with `turnComplete: false`.
- Keep edge creation idempotent and log dedupe decisions.
- Record the gesture/edge outcome for replay and debugging.

USER VALUE:
- The user can communicate by rearranging cards, not only by speaking, and the visible result does not depend on a model deciding to call another tool.
design:
Reference context shapes from the plan:
- Relational drag:
  `[CANVAS_UPDATE] User moved "Budget Risk" near "Timeline Risk". Distance: 30px. This appears intentional — interpret as a relationship signal and create a dependency edge.`
- Non-relational drag:
  `[CANVAS_UPDATE] User moved "Budget Risk" to (x, y).`
The backend should also persist the structural outcome directly rather than relying on the model to do it.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Dragging card A near card B within the threshold persists exactly one dependency edge, updates the dragged card position, injects relationship context, and shows the SVG edge immediately.
2. Dragging to empty space persists only the new position and injects the non-relational update path.
3. Repeating the same relational gesture does not create duplicate edges.
4. Replay/state history records both the move and any resulting edge creation.
5. Structured logs capture distance calculations, threshold decisions, dedupe outcomes, persisted IDs, and replay trigger metadata.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- The visible structural result must not depend on the model performing an undefined follow-up edge-creation action.
- Preserve enough metadata about the user gesture that replay/debug tooling can explain why an edge did or did not appear.

title: DL-2: Blog Post
description:
Write and publish blog post on dev.to per spec §14.1.
- Title: The Text Box Is Dead: What We Learned Building a Spatial Thinking Partner with Gemini
- ~700 words following spec §14.1 structure
- MUST include: This project was created for the purposes of entering the Gemini Live Agent Challenge hackathon.
- MUST include: #GeminiLiveAgentChallenge hashtag
- Embed architecture diagram
- Link to GitHub repo
- Must be PUBLIC (not unlisted/draft)
- +0.6 bonus points

Acceptance: Published on dev.to. Public URL. Contains required language and hashtag.
design:
Required blog structure from the plan:
1. The thesis: chat is 1D, canvas is 2D, and this is the CLI→GUI jump for AI interfaces.
2. The cognitive-science framing: whiteboards, war rooms, and spatial externalization as part of thought itself.
3. The technical stack: Gemini Live API, native audio, Proactive Audio, FunctionResponseScheduling, thinking budget, Cloud Run, Firestore, Google Search grounding, Calendar API, force layout, replay.
4. The hardest bug: NON_BLOCKING narration/speculation, the anti-narration system prompt, and the blocking-tool fallback that the shipped build relies on.
5. The moment it clicked: silent auto-crystallization creating cards without being asked.
6. What's next: where spatial AI interfaces go from here.
Required inclusions: the exact hackathon-disclosure sentence, `#GeminiLiveAgentChallenge`, embedded architecture diagram, and a repo link.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The post is published publicly on dev.to under the planned title or a close variant and has a stable public URL.
2. The body includes the required hackathon disclosure sentence and the `#GeminiLiveAgentChallenge` hashtag.
3. The post embeds the architecture diagram, links to the GitHub repo, and describes the shipped baseline honestly instead of overclaiming deferred upgrades.
4. The "hardest bug" section explicitly covers the NON_BLOCKING narration problem and the shipped blocking-tool fallback.
5. The post is strong enough to reuse in Devpost and judge follow-up without deadline-day rewriting.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Follow the full spec §14.1 outline explicitly: thesis, cognitive-science framing, technical stack, hardest bug, moment it clicked, what is next.
- The "hardest bug" section should specifically cover the NON_BLOCKING narration problem, the system-prompt guardrail, and the blocking-tool fallback that the shipped build uses.

title: CU-3: Ghost Card Animation
description:
Implement the ghost→solid creation animation that makes card creation feel deliberate, visible, and trustworthy instead of instantaneous and unexplained.

SCOPE:
- Render newly created cards in a ghost state at roughly 30% opacity with the documented shimmer/pulse treatment and no full drop shadow.
- Transition each card independently to the solid state after 300ms, with a 200ms ease-out visual settle.
- Preserve independent timers when multiple cards arrive in rapid succession; later cards must not inherit or reset earlier timers.
- Keep ghost-state styling/test hooks stable enough that force-layout, replay, and e2e suites can observe the transition reliably.
- Ensure the animation composes cleanly with synthesis styling and card reuse across restore/replay flows.

USER VALUE:
- Silent card creation feels intentional and legible, which is one of the signature magic moments of the product.
design:
Visual contract from the plan:
- Ghost: ~30% opacity, subtle shimmer/pulse, no full shadow.
- Solid: 100% opacity, drop shadow, 200ms ease-out transition.
- Each card owns its own 300ms solidification timer starting from the frontend notification time.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A newly created card renders in the ghost state immediately and transitions to the solid state after roughly 300ms.
2. Multiple cards created in rapid succession each keep their own independent ghost timer.
3. Ghost and solid states expose stable CSS classes/test hooks for unit and browser verification.
4. The transition composes cleanly with synthesis styling and does not remount or lose the artifact identity.
5. The animation remains visually clear at demo zoom without creating visible jitter or timing glitches.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- The ghost-card effect is not cosmetic fluff; it is part of the visible-thinking story and must therefore be deterministic enough for replay, e2e tests, and demo recording.
- Downstream force-layout logic should treat ghost participation intentionally rather than assuming ghost cards are already settled artifacts.

title: EX-1: Google Calendar Integration
description:
Implement the Google Calendar integration used by the live tool path and the visible calendar confirmation artifact.

SCOPE:
- Accept `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`, `GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URI`, and `GOOGLE_REFRESH_TOKEN` from the environment.
- Build the OAuth2 client with explicit refresh-token handling and clear separation between refresh failures and event-insert failures.
- Validate/normalize RFC3339 datetimes before calling Calendar.
- Implement `createEvent(title, start_time, end_time, description, attendees)` and return `{ event_id, status: "created", calendar_link }` as a plain object.
- Create the corresponding confirmation artifact on the canvas as part of the handler contract.
- Emit structured logs for token refresh, request shape, returned event IDs/links, and error conditions.

USER VALUE:
- Calendar actions feel real and visible, with on-canvas confirmation instead of invisible side effects.
design:
Calendar contract from the plan:
- Pre-authorized test account for demo use.
- Environment-driven OAuth credentials.
- Confirmation card is part of the visible UX, not an optional add-on.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Valid calendar-event requests call Google Calendar with normalized RFC3339 datetimes and return a plain-object response containing `event_id`, `status: "created"`, and `calendar_link`.
2. Token-refresh failures and event-insert failures are handled/logged distinctly.
3. A confirmation artifact is created on the canvas as part of the successful handler path.
4. Mocked/unit coverage can run without the human OAuth checkpoint, while the live integration bead remains blocked on that checkpoint.
5. Structured logs capture request timing, refresh behavior, returned event IDs/links, and failure reasons.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Validate and normalize RFC3339 datetimes before calling Google Calendar.
- Handle token refresh explicitly and log refresh failures distinctly from event-insert failures.
- The human OAuth checkpoint blocks live integration verification, not handler implementation or mocked unit coverage.
- Confirmation-card creation should be part of the handler contract, not left as an implied follow-up.

title: CU-11: Light/Dark Theme Toggle
description:
Implement the deferred theme-switching feature from spec §4.3 / §11 without degrading the strong dark-theme default used in the demo.

SCOPE:
- Add a clear theme toggle control for light and dark modes.
- Persist the selected theme at the workspace level so reloads preserve the user’s choice.
- Ensure cards, edges, replay UI, transcript panel, controls, and status indicators all honor the active theme tokens.
- Preserve the dark theme as the default demo aesthetic while making light mode polished rather than incidental.
- Keep contrast and readability high in both themes.

ACCEPTANCE:
1. Users can switch between dark and light themes at runtime.
2. The selected theme persists across reloads for the same workspace.
3. Core canvas surfaces and overlays restyle consistently in both themes.
4. No demo-critical UI becomes unreadable in either theme.
5. Theme changes are replay-safe and do not corrupt artifact state.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Users can switch between dark and light themes at runtime from a clear control.
2. The selected theme persists per workspace across reload/restore.
3. Cards, edges, replay UI, transcript, controls, and status indicators all restyle consistently in both themes.
4. Theme changes do not make demo-critical UI unreadable or corrupt replay/artifact state.
5. Any implementation extends UI/e2e coverage with structured logs or snapshots for theme switching, persistence, and contrast-sensitive surfaces.

title: CU-2: ArtifactCard Component
description:
Build the reusable card component that renders the core artifact types users read, drag, and trust on the canvas.

FILES:
- components/ArtifactCard.tsx
- supporting styling in the shared card/canvas styling layer

SCOPE:
- Render the baseline text-artifact surfaces used for normal cards, research-result cards, and calendar confirmation cards.
- Support the spec status treatments: `default`, `risk`, `question`, `done`, and `synthesis`.
- Render title, body, tags, optional type/status badges, and any concise metadata needed by research/calendar cards without creating one-off ad hoc components for simple card variants.
- Respect the shared canvas coordinate system and expose stable DOM hooks (`id`, `data-id`, `data-status`, `data-testid`) so force layout, replay, drag logic, and tests can target cards reliably.
- Support ghost/solid state transitions, synthesis styling hooks, and drag affordances/callbacks without remounting the artifact unnecessarily.
- Keep sizing and typography readable at normal demo zoom levels.

USER VALUE:
- Users can read and manipulate artifact cards quickly, and different kinds of agent output still feel like part of one coherent visual system.
design:
Rendering contract:
- Job artifacts have their own dedicated component; most other visible artifact types should still feel like cards within one visual family.
- Status/type metadata should enrich the card rather than turning it into a mini form.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The component renders title, body, tags, position, and status styling correctly for the baseline card variants.
2. Risk, question, done, and synthesis states are visually distinct without breaking layout or drag behavior.
3. Research-result and calendar-confirmation cards can reuse the component with small variant metadata instead of requiring separate card primitives.
4. Stable DOM/test hooks exist for force-layout targeting, replay interpolation, drag logic, and e2e assertions.
5. The card remains readable and draggable at normal canvas zoom levels and cooperates with ghost/synthesis styling.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Keep ArtifactCard broad enough to cover the non-job artifact variants the demo needs: ordinary thought cards, synthesis cards, research findings, and calendar confirmation cards.
- Preserve stable artifact identity/DOM hooks even as ghost-state, synthesis-state, replay, and drag behaviors layer on top.

title: CU-8: Connection Status Indicator
description:
Render the visible connection-state indicator that tells users whether the live system is healthy, recovering, or unavailable.

FILES:
- components/ConnectionStatusIndicator.tsx or the equivalent indicator surface inside the existing controls/canvas layer

SCOPE:
- Render the exact user-visible states from the plan: Connecting, Connected, Reconnecting, and Error.
- Drive the indicator from backend-authored connection/session lifecycle events rather than optimistic frontend guesses.
- Use the specified red-state guidance when recovery fails: the live API may be unavailable and the user should refresh or try again shortly.
- Keep the indicator legible without overpowering the canvas.
- Expose stable selectors/hooks so unit/e2e/deploy-smoke tests can assert specific states and messages.

USER VALUE:
- Users can tell the difference between “working,” “recovering,” and “dead,” which is essential for trust in a live voice app.
design:
Connection-state contract:
- Connecting: pulsing while the initial handshake is in progress.
- Connected: clearly healthy.
- Reconnecting: visible recovery in progress.
- Error: clear failure state plus refresh/try-again guidance, while preserving the current canvas/transcript.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The indicator shows Connecting, Connected, Reconnecting, and Error using the intended visual treatments.
2. State transitions are driven by real backend/socket/session events, not only local optimistic heuristics.
3. The Error state shows the intended user-facing guidance while leaving the canvas/transcript visible.
4. The indicator remains visible and readable at demo scale without hijacking attention from the canvas.
5. Stable selectors/hooks exist for UI, e2e, and deployed-smoke assertions.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Match the exact visible states and user-facing error copy from spec §5.5, including the red-state guidance that the Live API may be temporarily unavailable.
- Drive the indicator from real backend/socket lifecycle events rather than frontend heuristics.

title: EX-3: Research Job Integration
description:
Wire the research-job tool path so background work is visible on the canvas from job start through result delivery or failure.

SCOPE:
- On `start_research_job`, create both the visible job-node artifact and a backing Job record, then return the blocking “started” response immediately.
- Kick off the async research runner after the tool response.
- On success, inject the documented `[SYSTEM]` research-results prompt via `sendClientContent`, update the job node and Job record to complete, and let findings re-enter the canvas as individual result cards.
- On failure, mark the job visibly failed, persist the failure outcome, and log the reason.
- Keep the job node visible through the full lifecycle so the user never loses track of background work.
- Emit structured logs for job IDs, state transitions, injected finding/source counts, result-card IDs, and failure reasons.

USER VALUE:
- Research feels like a visible collaborative process rather than a hidden async black box.
design:
Reference injected research-results prompt from the plan:
`[SYSTEM] Research job {job_id} completed. Topic: "...". Key findings: [...]. Sources: [...]. Create a card for EACH key finding using create_card with placement:"near_artifact" and near_artifact_id:"{job_node_id}". Each finding should be its own card so it enters the force simulation and finds its semantic place among existing cards. If any finding contradicts an existing card, use status:"risk". Announce the overall results to the user.`
acceptance_criteria:
1. `start_research_job` creates a visible running job node plus a backing Job record and returns the blocking started response immediately.
2. Successful research completion injects the results prompt, updates job state to complete, and enables one-card-per-finding cascading back onto the canvas.
3. Failed research updates the visible job state to failed and records/logs the failure reason.
4. Result cards can be traced back to the originating job ID.
5. Structured logs capture job lifecycle transitions, finding/source counts, result-card IDs, and failure reasons.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- Persist both the visible job-node artifact and a backing Job record so status transitions are queryable and replayable.
- Handle the failure path explicitly: failed research should update the job node, log the reason, and avoid leaving the canvas in a permanent running state.
- Result cards should be created near the job node first and then be allowed to join semantic force layout rather than remaining visually stranded.

title: CU-1: Canvas Container
description:
Build the main spatial canvas surface that the rest of the product treats as the primary interface rather than a chat transcript.

FILES:
- components/Canvas.tsx
- the shared styling layer that establishes the canvas background, coordinate transforms, and overlay stacking

SCOPE:
- Render an absolutely positioned canvas surface using CSS transforms rather than a `<canvas>` element.
- Establish the shared coordinate system from the spec: `(0,0)` is the viewport center and the visible working area is approximately 1200×800 around that origin.
- Support panning and zooming with intentional, stable interactions that do not interfere with card dragging or overlay controls.
- Maintain viewport state (`center`, `zoom`, `size`) so backend state-injection/replay beads can reuse the same values instead of inventing their own model.
- Provide the visual/layering foundation for cards, SVG edges, replay UI, transcript panel, connection indicator, and controls.
- Keep the dark demo-default aesthetic while leaving the surface structurally ready for the later theme-toggle upgrade.
- Expose stable DOM/test hooks for viewport state, pan/zoom transforms, and child placement.

USER VALUE:
- The product feels like a real spatial workspace, not a pile of absolutely positioned divs.
- Spatial references, layout physics, replay, and drag interactions all share one coherent coordinate system.
design:
Canvas contract to preserve from the plan:
- Absolutely positioned DOM cards + CSS transforms + SVG edges; not a bitmap canvas.
- `(0,0)` is the semantic center of the working area and the force simulation centers on the same origin.
- Viewport metadata should be emitted in the same shape later used by `[CANVAS_STATE]` and replay/snapshot features.
acceptance_criteria:
1. An empty canvas renders with the intended spatial-workspace treatment and the dark demo-default aesthetic.
2. Panning and zooming work without breaking child placement, drag interactions, or overlay UI.
3. A child placed at `(0,0)` appears at the viewport center and the visible area behaves consistently with the ~1200×800 shared coordinate model.
4. Viewport center/zoom/size state is available in a form downstream beads can reuse for canvas-state injection, replay, restore, and snapshot grounding.
5. Stable selectors/test hooks exist for pan/zoom state, viewport transforms, and positioned child rendering.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- This bead must establish the shared coordinate truth used everywhere else: live tool placement, state injection, force layout, replay, topology, and on-demand snapshot grounding.
- Keep overlay layering intentional so status UI, controls, transcript, replay scrubber, and SVG edges can coexist without pointer-event chaos.

title: CU-10: Research Job Node Component
description:
Build the dedicated research job artifact UI described in spec §§7.3, 11.1, and 13.2 Shot 3/4 so research work is visible, legible, and trustworthy to users.

FILES:
- components/JobNode.tsx
- supporting styles in the existing canvas/card styling layer

SCOPE:
- Render a distinct job-node artifact for type:"job" that is visually different from normal cards.
- Status variants: running, complete, failed.
- Running state: pulsing animation, active indicator, concise subtitle such as "Researching...".
- Complete state: pulse stops, completion badge appears, summary/count metadata can render.
- Failed state: error styling and short failure message.
- Render title/topic, current status, timestamps or duration metadata when available, and finding/source counts after completion.
- Expose stable DOM hooks (data-id, data-status, data-testid) so force-layout code, replay, and e2e tests can target the node reliably.
- Respect the shared canvas coordinate system and drag model so the node can participate in layout/replay like other artifacts.
- Support ghost-to-solid behavior when the node is first created, but keep the running pulse active after solidification until the job completes.
- Ensure the component reads cleanly at demo zoom levels and remains visually distinct from synthesis cards and calendar confirmation cards.

USER-FOCUSED BEHAVIOR:
- Users should be able to tell at a glance that background work has started, is still active, and has completed.
- The component should communicate progress without requiring audio.
- Failure should be visible on-canvas instead of disappearing into logs.
acceptance_criteria:
1. JobNode.tsx renders running, complete, and failed variants for type: job artifacts.
2. Running nodes visibly pulse after ghost solidification until completion.
3. Complete and failed transitions update styles in place without remounting the artifact.
4. The node can be positioned, dragged, replayed, and targeted by SVG edges like any other artifact.
5. Stable selectors exist for unit/e2e tests and DOM-based layout logic.
6. Mock rendering demonstrates legible UI at normal canvas zoom.

title: IN-5B: Calendar Live Integration
description:
Implement the live calendar flow as its own demo-critical integration bead so the human OAuth checkpoint only blocks the calendar path, not unrelated research work.

SCOPE:
- Wire `create_calendar_event` from the live session through the backend handler to Google Calendar using the checkpoint-backed credentials.
- Ensure successful event creation returns `event_id`, `calendar_link`, and a visible confirmation artifact on the canvas.
- Keep the user-facing confirmation brief and grounded in the already-completed visible side effect.
- Surface refresh-token failures, event-insert failures, and malformed datetime requests visibly and with structured diagnostics.
- Preserve replay/history/persistence for the confirmation artifact so the scheduling action remains visible after reload and in the replay closer.
- Emit structured logs for request timing, normalized datetimes, token refresh behavior, event IDs/links, confirmation-artifact IDs, and failure reasons.

USER VALUE:
- Scheduling an action feels real, visible, and trustworthy instead of disappearing into a backend side effect.
- Calendar-specific credential work no longer blocks unrelated demo-critical research integration.
acceptance_criteria:
1. `create_calendar_event` works end to end with the human-provided OAuth credentials and returns `event_id`, `status: "created"`, and `calendar_link`.
2. A successful calendar call creates a visible confirmation artifact that persists, replays, and restores correctly.
3. The live flow yields a brief user-facing confirmation without narrating tool execution.
4. Token-refresh failures, insert failures, and datetime-validation failures remain visible and are logged distinctly.
5. The flow is covered by the existing mocked/unit/e2e verification plan, with structured logs rich enough to diagnose credential vs API vs app failures.
notes:
PLAN REVISION:
- This bead exists specifically to stop the human OAuth checkpoint from blocking research integration.
- Keep the visible confirmation artifact as part of the live success path so the scheduling action remains demonstrable even if audio is hard to hear.
- Reuse the existing verification beads: mocked unit coverage lands before the checkpoint, then live e2e/deployed verification closes this integration bead once credentials exist.

title: AU-2: Backend WebSocket Server
description:
Create the Bun-based WebSocket server that proxies between browser and Gemini. Per spec §5.1.
- Express or raw HTTP server in app/api/ or standalone server file
- WebSocket upgrade handler (use ws library)
- Accept browser connections
- Define explicit browser/backend message types: health, audio_in, audio_out, transcript, tool_result, canvas_update, connection_state, error, and heartbeat/ping
- Health check endpoint
- Environment variable loading from .env.local
acceptance_criteria:
1. The server starts under `bun run dev`, not npm.
2. A browser client can connect, exchange heartbeat/ping traffic, and send/receive typed or audio session messages.
3. Health checks and connection_state/error messages work end-to-end.
